textPlain: What an amazing construction. It reminds me of a story from the metallurgist who visited the Breakers, the elaborate mansion complex of the Vanderbilt family, and on a tour was shown the Morning Room. The guide said the brilliant silver-white walls were silver plate. “How do you keep it from tarnishing?” He asked. The guide didn’t know, so the scientist asked for permission to test it.Stories vary on whether he tested it or someone stopped him before taking a sample, but all agree on the technology used: that’s not silver. They coated an entire room with platinum.
I know it's supposed to be beautiful but to me it just looks like a gaudy mess. An assault on my eyes.

I find it extraordinary that it took only 10 years for early 18th century craftsmen to make it, but it required 24 years to reconstruct despite having access to late 20th century tools and technology.
The reconstruction of 8 wonder of the World is splendid
It's the maximalist aesthetic. You probably prefer minimalism.Does anyone know a monarch that eschewed the opulent trappings of his/her ancestors in favor of minimalism?
It is most likely something you need to experience in real life in order to appreciate the beauty.The smell alone must be amazing.
What late 20th century tools and technology were used to reconstruct it that weren’t available during its construction?
